name: Bardenas Reales Loop from Tudela
name_original: Tudela - Valareña - Santa Engracia – vuelta por el Parque Natural de las Bardenas Reales
description: Cycle 54.0 miles (86.9 km) through the unique semi-desert landscape of Bardenas Reales Natural Park on this moderate road cycling route.
region_intro: Road cycling around Tudela offers diverse terrain, from the semi-desert landscapes of Bardenas Reales Natural Park to the fertile plains along the Ebro River. The region features well-developed cycling infrastructure, including greenways built on former railway lines. Cyclists can explore routes through agricultural lands, along canals, and into surrounding hills, providing varied experiences. The area's physical features include eroded sandstone formations, riverine paths, and gentle to challenging ascents. Best road cycling routes around Tudela The most popular road cycling route is Corella - Fitero - Grávalos – circular, a 55.7 miles (89.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 54 minutes to complete. This moderate route loops through cultural towns with riverside views. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Bardenas Reales Loop from Tudela, a moderate 54.0 miles (86.9 km) path. This route explores the unique semi-desert landscape of Bardenas Reales Natural Park. Local road cyclists also love the Cornago – Fitero loop from Tudela de Navarra, a 70.5 miles (113.4 km) trail leading through the surrounding hills with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 5 hours 35 minutes. Road cycling around Tudela is defined by the Ebro River, Bardenas Reales Natural Park, and agricultural lands. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy greenways to challenging climbs. The routes in Tudela are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 29 reviews. More than 1300 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Tudela's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Tudela?
A: Road cycling around Tudela offers a diverse range of landscapes. You can experience the unique semi-desert formations of the Bardenas Reales Natural Park, cycle along the fertile plains of the Ebro River, or explore routes through agricultural lands and canals. There are also routes that venture into the surrounding hills, providing varied experiences from flat greenways to challenging ascents.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Tudela?
A: Yes, Tudela offers routes suitable for all skill levels. For beginners and families, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40332512" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tarazonica Greenway&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent option. Built on a former railway line, it provides a traffic-free, gentle, and flat ride through pleasant agricultural landscapes, making it ideal for an easy and enjoyable experience.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders near Tudela?
A: Experienced riders seeking a challenge will find several demanding routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31482576" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cornago – Fitero loop from Tudela de Navarra&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 70.5-mile (113.4 km) trail with significant elevation gain. For an even greater challenge, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24560480" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Buste Viewpoint – Bar de Moncayo loop from Tudela de Navarra&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 141.9 km with over 2,000m of elevation gain.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the road cycling routes?
A: The region boasts striking natural features. The Bardenas Reales Natural Park, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, offers unique lunar-like landscapes with eroded clay, gypsum, and sandstone formations. Along the Ebro River, you'll find rich flora and fauna. You can also visit natural monuments like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/945742" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castildetierra&lt;/a&gt; or viewpoints such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1170994" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de las Cortinillas&lt;/a&gt;. Several routes also pass by beautiful lakes, including &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5187852" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cortinas Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5599931" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zapata Pond&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: When is the best time of year for road cycling in Tudela?
A: The best time for road cycling in Tudela is generally between April and June. During these months, daylight hours are longer, and temperatures are pleasant, making for comfortable rides. The Ebro River is also at its most splendid during this period. Cycling on weekdays is often recommended, especially in areas like Bardenas Reales, to avoid dust from cars.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available around Tudela?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Tudela are circular, allowing you to start and end in the same location. An example is the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984637956" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corella - Fitero - Grávalos – circular&lt;/a&gt; route, which loops through cultural towns with riverside views. The popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e985022042" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bardenas Reales Loop from Tudela&lt;/a&gt; is also a circular path exploring the semi-desert landscape.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available in the Tudela region?
A: There are over 40 road cycling routes available in the Tudela region, catering to various preferences and skill levels. These include 9 easy routes, 22 moderate routes, and 11 difficult routes, ensuring options for everyone from casual riders to seasoned cyclists.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Tudela?
A: The road cycling routes in Tudela are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 29 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, from the unique Bardenas Reales to the scenic Ebro River paths, and the well-developed cycling infrastructure that makes exploring the region enjoyable.

Q: Are there amenities like cafes or accommodation near the cycling routes in Tudela?
A: Tudela serves as a convenient base with various amenities for cyclists. The city offers services and routes that allow for exploration. Many accommodations in Tudela also collaborate with e-bike rental services, which can be useful for exploring areas like Bardenas Natural Park. You'll find opportunities to combine cycling with exploring cultural towns and enjoying local cuisine.

Q: How can I access the road cycling routes, and is parking available?
A: Tudela is a central point for accessing many routes. While specific parking details for every trailhead are not provided, the city itself offers amenities. Many routes, like the Tarazonica Greenway, start directly from or near Tudela, making them accessible. Public transport options to Tudela are also available, allowing you to start your cycling adventure from the city.
tips: This stretch of the Tudela-Ejea highway crosses the Bardenas Reales Natural Park for about 15 km. The landscape is truly marvelous, not as impressive as in the northern part of the park where the desert takes center stage, but here you will still be able to see some glimpses of what this wonderful landscape is.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like for cycling this route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily designed for racebikes, featuring mostly well-paved surfaces that are generally easy to ride. However, as it passes through the Bardenas Reales Natural Park, be aware that some sections within the park, especially if you venture off the main path, might include unpaved or less-maintained surfaces. A general cycling route in the Bardenas Reales can be around 69% asphalt and 30% gravel, so be prepared for varied conditions.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect on this ride?
faqs.answer: You'll experience a truly unique and captivating 'lunar' landscape within the Bardenas Reales Natural Park. Expect dramatic, eroded geological formations like canyons, plateaus, and 'cabezos' (isolated hills). The park's semi-desert environment offers an otherworldly backdrop, distinct from much of Spain, with ever-changing photographic opportunities depending on the light.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This moderate road cycling route usually takes about 3 hours and 28 minutes to complete. This duration is an estimate and can vary based on your fitness level, speed, and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What are the main sights or landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: The primary highlight is cycling through the unique landscape of the Road through the Bardenas Reales. Within the Natural Park, you might spot famous geological formations like Castildetierra, a distinctive natural tower, and the 'badlands' of Blanquizales de Pisquerra. The entire park itself is a landmark, offering spectacular views.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during spring (April-May) or autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder. Summers can be extremely hot in the Bardenas Reales, requiring significant hydration and early starts. Winters can be cold, though cycling is generally possible, just be prepared for cooler conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for the Bardenas Reales Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no entrance fees or specific permits required for cycling the main public roads and tracks within the Bardenas Reales Natural Park. However, it's always wise to check the official park regulations before your visit, as access rules can change, especially concerning protected areas or specific events.

faqs.question: Where does the route start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route begins in Tudela. As a city, Tudela offers various parking options. You should be able to find parking within the city to start your ride conveniently. It's advisable to check for specific parking areas near the initial segment of the route.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as 'moderate' and requires 'good fitness' due to its distance of nearly 87 kilometers and an elevation gain of about 390 meters. While there are no overly strenuous climbs, the length and potential for varied terrain mean it's not ideal for absolute beginners. Cyclists with some experience and a reasonable level of fitness will enjoy it most.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I encounter while cycling?
faqs.answer: The Bardenas Reales Natural Park is home to diverse flora and fauna adapted to its arid conditions. While cycling, you might spot various bird species, including impressive raptors like the Griffon Vulture and the Golden Eagle soaring overhead. Keep an eye out for other desert-adapted wildlife, though many animals are more active during cooler parts of the day.

faqs.question: Is the Tudela - Valareña - Santa Engracia cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a road cycling route, it's generally not suitable for bringing dogs along, especially given the distance and potential for high temperatures in the Bardenas Reales. While dogs might be allowed in certain areas of the Natural Park on a leash, the nature of this specific long-distance road ride makes it impractical and potentially unsafe for pets.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this ride?
faqs.answer: Given the length and the semi-desert environment, it's crucial to bring ample water and hydration supplies, as services within the Bardenas Reales Natural Park are limited. Sun protection (sunscreen, hat, sunglasses), snacks, basic bike repair tools, and a fully charged phone are also highly recommended. Dress in layers suitable for the weather, especially considering potential temperature fluctuations.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other paths. You'll find sections that coincide with the Camino de la Vera Cruz (El camino de los Templarios), the Camino natural de Ebro - Tramo central, the Corredor Verde del Ebro, and the Camino Jacobeo del Ebro, among others.
